chunk_summary: Guidelines for discharge decisions in outpatient CMAM programs in India when MUAC and WHZ criteria disagree, emphasizing admission criteria, edema-free stability, and functional recovery to ensure safe outcomes.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of MUAC and WHZ in identifying high-risk SAM cases and their discordance during recovery.
Learn the criteria for safe discharge in CMAM programs, including MUAC ≥125 mm and edema-free stability.
Recognize the importance of functional recovery and caregiver competence in discharge decisions.
Identify potential causes of discordant recovery, such as chronic infection or malabsorption.
key_facts: MUAC <115 mm, WHZ/WLZ <-3, or nutritional edema are key admission criteria for CMAM programs.
Safe discharge requires MUAC ≥125 mm and no bilateral pitting edema for at least two consecutive visits, or WHZ/WLZ ≥-2 and no edema for two consecutive visits.
Do not discharge if only one indicator normalizes; residual muscle wasting (e.g., MUAC <125 mm) carries significant mortality risk.
Functional recovery includes sustained appetite, activity, and caregiver competence in safe feeding practices.
In India, documenting both MUAC and weight-for-height during clinic visits reduces the risk of premature discharge.
reference_quote: A safe WHO recovery standard is: MUAC ≥125 mm and no bilateral pitting edema for at least two consecutive visits, or WHZ/WLZ ≥-2 and no edema for at least two consecutive visits, depending on the program pathway.
document_key_topics: MUAC and WHZ discordance in recovery
